title: Agentic Coding: How I 10x’d My Development Workflow
author: Nicolas
text:
Agentic Coding: How I 10x’d My Development Workflow
As part of this new article, I would love to share something that has completely transformed my development workflow over the past six months. While I primarily work as a consultant and architect in the GenAI space, I wouldn’t call myself a ‘proper’ developer. Yet, I recently built and launched my side project echodocs.ai, a complete SaaS AI platform, from the ground up — NextJS, serverless architecture, PostgreSQL database, the whole package.
The secret? Coding agents, particularly Cursor. And the results have been nothing short of transformative.
What’s fascinating is how these tools have evolved from simple code completion to becoming genuine development partners. For those who’ve been following my journey, you know I’m all about finding that sweet spot between technical depth and practical application. Today’s post is exactly that — a deep dive into how I’ve leveraged agentic coding to amplify my capabilities, with concrete examples and actionable insights.
From implementing smart prompting strategies to mastering context awareness, I’ll share the exact methods that took me from struggling with basic components to confidently building complex features. These aren’t just theoretical concepts — they’re battle-tested approaches that helped me ship a real product.
Ready to transform your development workflow? Let’s dive in.
What is an coding agent?
Think of a coding agent as your super-powered IDE companion — but it’s much more than just autocomplete on steroids. While you might be familiar with pasting code into ChatGPT for suggestions, coding agents operate on a fundamentally different level.
Unlike simple LLM prompting where you ask questions and get responses, an agent is deeply integrated into your development environment. It can:
- Read your entire codebase in real-time
- Access your file system directly
- Execute commands in your terminal
- Create, modify, and delete files
- Run tests and debug code
- Interface with git operations
Besides the autonomous manner in which they operate, what sets agents apart are three key capabilities:
- Function Calling: They can execute specific actions like creating files, running tests, or modifying code
- Tool Integration: They interact with your development environment, version control, and other tools
- Memory & Context: They maintain state and understanding across your entire development session
The real magic happens when these capabilities combine with context awareness. Your agent understands your project structure, dependencies, and coding patterns — making it feel like a teammate who’s been with the project from day one. Instead of copy-pasting snippets back and forth, you’re having a dynamic interaction within your actual development environment.
Remember that viral Devin AI demo that broke the internet? It showcased what’s possible when you push these capabilities to their limits:
Types of agents
In my experience, coding agents typically fall into three categories:
Simple Agents with Tools
These are general-purpose agents equipped with programming-specific tools and function calling capabilities. They work independently of any IDE but can interact with your development environment through their tool set.
Key capabilities:
- Function calling for code operations
- Access to development tools and APIs
- Code analysis and generation
- Multi-agent coordination potential
- File and system operations via tools
Notable examples:
- GPT-4 with Code Interpreter: Powerful code execution and analysis
- Claude with tool-calling: Strong at code understanding and generation
Integrated IDE Agents
This is where the magic happens with direct IDE integration. These agents understand your project context and can manipulate code in real-time.
Key capabilities:
- Deep project context understanding
- Real-time code suggestions
- Direct file system access
- Refactoring and code organization
- Multi-file operations
Popular tools:
- Cursor (my go-to): Standalone IDE with exceptional context understanding
- Continue: VS Code extension with impressive task completion
- GitHub Copilot: Tight VS Code integration with strong suggestions
Full-Stack Development Agents
The most comprehensive category — these platforms aim to handle your entire development pipeline, from architecture to deployment.
Key capabilities:
- End-to-end development support
- Architecture planning
- CI/CD integration
- Testing and deployment
- Project management features
Available platforms:
- Replit: Complete development environment with AI integration
- bolt.new: Focused on rapid application development
- Lovable.dev: Emphasizes code quality and maintenance
Through my experience, I’ve found that combining a simple agent’s flexibility with an integrated IDE agent’s project awareness gives the best results. For instance, I often use GPT-4 or even Perplexity for high-level planning and architecture discussions, then switch to Cursor for actual implementation.
The key isn’t to pick the most feature-rich tool, but to understand how different types of agents can complement each other in your workflow. This mixed approach has been crucial in my productivity gains.
My Optimized Workflow with Coding Agents
After months of experimentation, I’ve developed a workflow that significantly boosts my productivity. While I’ll use Cursor in my examples, these principles apply to most coding agents.
Never Start Empty
Here’s something I learned the hard way: coding agents are like junior developers — they need context to shine. Starting with a blank slate limits their effectiveness dramatically.
What I do instead:
- Always initialize a basic project structure
- Install core dependencies upfront
- Set up basic configuration files
- Include essential framework setup
For example, with a Next.js project:
# At minimum, initialize your project
npx create-next-app@latest
# Set up essential dependencies
npm install your-core-packages
# Establish basic structure
mkdir -p src/{components,lib,types}
This approach is particularly important because most models powering these agents have knowledge cutoffs. They might not know about the latest framework versions or library updates. Giving them a proper foundation helps bridge this gap.
The Power of .cursorrules
One of the most impactful features I’ve discovered in Cursor is the .cursorrules file. Think of it as your project’s AI constitution — it defines how the AI should behave and what rules it should follow.
# .cursorrules
language: typescript
framework: next.js
conventions:
- 'Use named exports over default exports'
- 'Follow atomic design for components'
- 'Implement error boundaries at page level'
standards:
- 'Write unit tests for utility functions'
- 'Use React Query for data fetching'
architecture:
- 'Follow feature-based folder structure'
Why this matters:
- Creates customized AI behavior specific to your project
- Ensures consistency across code generation
- Provides crucial context about your project’s architecture
- Speeds up development by reducing manual corrections
- Helps maintain team alignment on coding standards
- Embeds project-specific knowledge
Pro tip: Check out the awesome-cursorrules repository for pre-made rules based on your tech stack.
Prompting for Code
While LLMs and coding agents are getting better at understanding intent, investing time in proper prompting still pays huge dividends. Here’s what works for me:
✅ Effective prompts:
- Start with the desired outcome
- Specify technical constraints upfront
- Reference existing patterns in your codebase
- Break complex requests into smaller tasks
"Create a new component for user profile display.
- Follow our atomic design pattern
- Use Tailwind for styling
- Include loading and error states
- Reference existing component at @components/cards/BaseCard.tsx"
❌ Less effective prompts:
- Vague feature requests
- Multiple unrelated changes in one prompt
- Assuming the agent knows project-specific details
- Overly complex conditional logic in one go
"Make a profile page that looks good and works well"
Sudo Code Approach
One technique that’s dramatically improved my results is what I call the “sudo code first” approach. Instead of trying to explain complex requirements in prompts, I create a skeleton structure with comments:
- First, lay out your structure:
/features
/auth
LoginForm.tsx
authService.ts
types.ts
2. Then add sudo code comments:
// LoginForm.tsx
/*
- Form component with email/password fields
- Handles validation
- Calls authService.login
- Shows error/success states
*/
// authService.ts
/*
- login(email, password) function
- Returns JWT token
- Handles error cases
- Stores token in localStorage
*/
This approach helps the agent understand the broader context and prevents it from creating unnecessary files or going off in unexpected directions.
Strategic References
Once you have your foundation, being selective about context becomes crucial. Using Cursor’s “@” reference feature (similar features exist in other tools) lets you precisely control what the agent sees:
@components/shared/Button.tsx - for styling consistency
@lib/api/types.ts - for type definitions
@features/auth/authService.ts - for authentication logic
This is particularly powerful when you want to:
- Rewrite existing code
- Create new features that match existing patterns
- Ensure consistency across components
Documentation is key
I’ve learned that documentation isn’t just for humans — it’s a crucial communication channel with your coding agent. I maintain a robust /docs folder with markdown files:
# Authentication Flow
- JWT-based auth
- Token storage in httpOnly cookies
- Refresh token rotation every 7 days
# API Structure
- REST endpoints under /api/v1
- GraphQL endpoint at /api/graphql
In Cursor, you can reference these docs directly with “@Docs”, which helps the agent maintain context. Even better, you can ask the agent to update documentation as your code evolves, keeping everything in sync.
This documentation-first approach has two major benefits:
- It helps the agent understand your codebase’s bigger picture
- It keeps your documentation current as you develop
Conclusion
After spending months refining these workflows and techniques, I’ve learned that coding agents aren’t just tools — they’re collaborators that grow more valuable as you learn to work with them effectively. The key isn’t to view them as magic solutions that write perfect code, but as powerful assistants that amplify your capabilities when given the right context and direction.
The most important insights I’ve gained:
- Context is king — the more structured information you provide, the better the output
- Investment in setup (like .cursorrules and documentation) pays massive dividends
- Mixing techniques (sudo code + strategic references + documentation) creates a multiplier effect
- The agent is only as good as your ability to guide it
Think of these practices as building blocks — you don’t need to implement everything at once. Start with one technique, perhaps the sudo code approach or strategic references, and build from there. As you get comfortable, layer in more sophisticated practices like comprehensive .cursorrules files and documentation integration.
Remember, the landscape of coding agents is evolving rapidly. The tools and capabilities available today will likely be dramatically different in six months. However, these fundamental principles of context, structure, and clear communication will remain valuable regardless of how the technology evolves.
The goal isn’t to replace traditional coding practices but to augment them with AI capabilities in a way that feels natural and productive. When done right, it’s like having a skilled pair programmer who’s always available, infinitely patient, and gets better at understanding your needs over time.
I advocate for openness and transparency in the use of generative AI (GenAI) . While all the ideas, thoughts, and concepts shared here are genuinely crafted and curated by me, I do use GenAI-based technology to enhance, refine, and optimize the presentation of this content, including this very text.
